subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Gravitational Waves
description: A short lesson to help you apply Gravitational Waves.

hook.text: Gravitational waves are ripples in spacetime caused by massive objects accelerating—like two black holes spiraling together.

buildup.text: Einstein predicted them in 1916, but they're incredibly faint. Detecting them seemed impossible for nearly a century.

discovery.text: LIGO detected the first gravitational wave in 2015—from two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away.

twist.text: LIGO measures distortions smaller than a proton. Its lasers detect length changes of one ten-thousandth of a proton.

climax.text: Gravitational wave astronomy opened a new window on the universe. We can now 'hear' events invisible to telescopes.

punchline.text: Ripples in spacetime itself. A hundred years from prediction to proof.

quiz.question: What was the source of the first gravitational wave detected by LIGO?
options: Two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away
A supernova explosion in a nearby galaxy
The Big Bang's initial expansion of spacetime
